messages.author: aka47
messages.text: Yes indeed they are, I started off with a pair of Heltec Wireless Stick Lites (still runing them) with a copper wire slim jim homebrew antenna and have upgraded to a pair of antennas a Omni and a Yagi, the Yagi is pointing BradHudersFax way to link into the mesh groups linking up through there and the Omni is to serve local folks. I like the WSL lites as they are minimalist and have RF cans on them to help reduce noise etc, good for infrastructure kit.

embeds_html:
SenseCAP Solar Node P1-Pro for Meshtastic
It is a solar-powered communication node that integrates the XIAO nRF52840 Plus main controller, the Wio-SX1262 LoRa module, and the XIAO L76K GPS module. It is specifically designed for areas without network coverage. With a built-in battery, it supports long-distance communication, precise positioning, and low-power operation. It's suitable fo...

messages.author: CoBoL
messages.text: Hopefully they are working, one as a base station the other as a repeater with a outside external antenna on my roof. Based around the Keighley/Steaton area. "M7DBP-Base" is my base station and "M7DBP-Repeater". I am receiving some msg on public and gaining a few contacts. But how can I tell if the repeater is doing its job? Only time linked to computer was initial setup via USB, now this is disconnected and the device is in place. Thanks Dave (M7DBP)
messages.content_html: Hopefully they are working, one as a base station the other as a repeater with a outside external antenna on my roof. Based around the Keighley/Steaton area. messages.author: silverphish 🌈
messages.text: If you're talking a heltec v3, thats esp32 so it'll be power hungry. Power usage will vary but for a vague estimate, I typically go for the worse case. In this case Heltec says a v3 uses 230mA. Assuming 3.3v, thats 3.3 * 0.230 = 0.759w per second. Assuming the battery can handle 3.3v, thats 3.3 * 10,000mA = 33,000w So 33,00w / 0.759w = ~43,478 seconds = ~724 minutes = ~12 hours just off that battery alone
messages.content_html: If you're talking a heltec v3, thats esp32 so it'll be power hungry. Power usage will vary but for a vague estimate, I typically go for the worse case. In this case Heltec says a v3 uses 230mA. Assuming 3.3v, thats 3.3 * 0.230 = 0.759w per second. Assuming the battery can handle 3.3v, thats 3.3 * 10,000mA = 33,000w So 33,00w / 0.759w = ~43,478 seconds = ~724 minutes = ~12 hours just off that battery alone
messages.reply_html:
ProtocolXIX 🇬🇧
What sort of life will this get in a solar node with a heltec and the ab-iot signal booster

messages.author: likkleidiot_nwo
messages.text: These are some numbers for the quick idle power draws I did for some boards I had laying around last night and predicted run times on solar 10,000 mAh pack (~30 Wh usable) and a 5 V / 5 W panel in the Sheffield area (assumed peak-sun hours: winter≈1.0, spring≈2.5, autumn≈2.5, summer≈4.5; panel derated to ~3.5 W in sun). Heltec V3 (~2.9 W) Winter: ~10.9 h (~0.5 days) Spring: ~11.8 h (~0.5 days) Autumn: ~11.8 h (~0.5 days) Summer: ~13.4 h (~0.6 days) No solar (for reference): ~10.3 h LILYGO T3 S3 (~0.33 W) Winter: ~162.9 h (~6.8 days) Spring: sustainable (charging) Autumn: sustainable (charging) Summer: sustainable (charging) No solar: ~90.9 h XIAO ESP32-S3 (~0.537 W) Winter: ~76.7 h (~3.2 days) Spring: ~174.0 h (~7.2 days) Autumn: ~174.0 h (~7.2 days) Summer: sustainable (charging) No solar: ~55.9 h XIAO nRF52840 (~0.079 W) Winter: sustainable (charging) Spring: sustainable (charging) Autumn: sustainable (charging) Summer: sustainable (charging) No solar: ~379.7 h (~15.8 days)
messages.content_html: These are some numbers for the quick idle power draws I did for some boards I had laying around last night and predicted run times on solar 10,000 mAh pack (~30 Wh usable) and a 5 V / 5 W panel in the Sheffield area (assumed peak-sun hours: winter≈1.0, spring≈2.5, autumn≈2.5, summer≈4.5; panel derated to ~3.5 W in sun). Heltec V3 (~2.9 W) Winter: ~10.9 h (~0.5 days) Spring: ~11.8 h (~0.5 days) Autumn: ~11.8 h (~0.5 days) Summer: ~13.4 h (~0.6 days) No solar (for reference): ~10.3 h LILYGO T3 S3 (~0.33 W) Winter: ~162.9 h (~6.8 days) Spring: sustainable (charging) Autumn: sustainable (charging) Summer: sustainable (charging) No solar: ~90.9 h XIAO ESP32-S3 (~0.537 W) Winter: ~76.7 h (~3.2 days) Spring: ~174.0 h (~7.2 days) Autumn: ~174.0 h (~7.2 days) Summer: sustainable (charging) No solar: ~55.9 h XIAO nRF52840 (~0.079 W) Winter: sustainable (charging) Spring: sustainable (charging) Autumn: sustainable (charging) Summer: sustainable (charging) No solar: ~379.7 h (~15.8 days)

If you're talking a heltec v3, thats esp32 so it'll be power hungry. Power usage will vary but for a vague estimate, I typically go for the worse case. In this case Heltec says a v3 uses 230mA. Assuming 3.3v, thats 3.3 * 0.230 = 0.759w per second. Assuming the battery can handle 3.3v, thats 3.3 * 10,000mA = 33,000w So 33,00w / 0.759w = ~43,478 seconds = ~724 minutes = ~12 hours just off that battery alone

messages.content_html: So, for a loft node. Whats the best hardware? I have a V3 what i plan on using for my mobile unit.

messages.author: Rich
messages.text: I like RAK units, but also had good luck with Heltec T114. If its going to be DC powered you can use anything. Decent antenna such as https://thepihut.com/products/lora-antenna-with-pigtail-868mhz-black if it doesn't need to be waterproof. Mount it high up and away from any metal.
messages.content_html: I like RAK units, but also had good luck with Heltec T114. If its going to be DC powered you can use anything. Decent antenna such as https://thepihut.com/products/lora-antenna-with-pigtail-868mhz-black if it doesn't need to be waterproof. Mount it high up and away from any metal.
embeds_html:
LoRa Antenna with Pigtail - 868MHz Black
This is a 170mm black 868MHz 50ohm antenna kit tested and recommended by iLabs for use with their LoRa products, such as the Challenger RP2040 LoRa. They can also be used with any other LoRa board requiring an 868MHz U.FL antenna solution.

messages.author: mowcius
messages.text: OK crew, I can feasibly set up a repeater in LS26 (not sure if @boychicken71 is still around, might also be good for @jamos9135 ) with LOS to the city centre straight down the valley, one in LS12 with LOS to the city centre down the valley the other way, both hardwired, and one in LS1 (no height, potentially has to be indoors, so might be a waste of time for now). Someone recommend me some gear, ideally less than £50 ea. all in. Both the LS26 and LS12 repeaters will be in the loft initially, tile and slate roofs, but both antennas will be able to live outside longer term, with around 1m of appropriate coax. Yagis might make sense for both longer term but for now I'll go omni as there's nobody specific I can point at.
messages.content_html: OK crew, I can feasibly set up a repeater in LS26 (not sure if @boychicken71 is still around, might also be good for @jamos9135) with LOS to the city centre straight down the valley, one in LS12 with LOS to the city centre down the valley the other way, both hardwired, and one in LS1 (no height, potentially has to be indoors, so might be a waste of time for now). Someone recommend me some gear, ideally less than £50 ea. all in. Both the LS26 and LS12 repeaters will be in the loft initially, tile and slate roofs, but both antennas will be able to live outside longer term, with around 1m of appropriate coax. Yagis might make sense for both longer term but for now I'll go omni as there's nobody specific I can point at.

messages.author: boychicken71
messages.text: @mowcius I am indeed still here with my 2 x Heltec V3's. I'm having very little success with these at all and have forgotten about them for several weeks. The only other device I've ever been able to contact is my repeater which is about 20 feet away so not a great result tbh. I've re-flashed them both to Meshcore 1.9.0 and configured on Narrow band now, both have some cheapo AliExpress 868MHz antenna that are about 12cm in length and cost less than a fiver. No idea if these are doing anything better, worse than stock or even work. I'd have put the repeater in the loft but there is no power up there. The best I can do at the moment is have them in different upstairs bedrooms near the window. Every day I send multiple test messages and flood pings but nothing, the best I get is "Heard 1 repeat" and that's from my own repeater!! Not sure what else I can do or if I'm doing something wrong or it's just a lack of other devices in the area?

messages.reply_html:
mowcius
OK crew, I can feasibly set up a repeater in LS26 (not sure if @boychicken71 is still around, might also be good for @jamos9135) with LOS to the city centre straight down the valley, one in LS12 with LOS to the city centre down the valley the other way, both hardwired, and one in LS1 (no height, potentially has to be indoors, so might be a waste of time for now). Someone recommend me some gear, ideally less than £50 ea. all in. Both the LS26 and LS12 repeaters will be in the loft initially, tile and slate roofs, but both antennas will be able to live outside longer term, with around 1m of appropriate coax. Yagis might make sense for both longer term but for now I'll go omni as there's nobody specific I can point at.

messages.author: mowcius
messages.text: I'll let you know once my gear arrives. At the very least we should be able to get talking to each other. Where in LS26 are you?
messages.content_html: I'll let you know once my gear arrives. At the very least we should be able to get talking to each other. Where in LS26 are you?

@mowcius I am indeed still here with my 2 x Heltec V3's. I'm having very little success with these at all and have forgotten about them for several weeks. The only other device I've ever been able to contact is my repeater which is about 20 feet away so not a great result tbh. I've re-flashed them both to Meshcore 1.9.0 and configured on Narrow band now, both have some cheapo AliExpress 868MHz antenna that are about 12cm in length and cost less than a fiver. No idea if these are doing anything better, worse than stock or even work. I'd have put the repeater in the loft but there is no power up there. The best I can do at the moment is have them in different upstairs bedrooms near the window. Every day I send multiple test messages and flood pings but nothing, the best I get is "Heard 1 repeat" and that's from my own repeater!! Not sure what else I can do or if I'm doing something wrong or it's just a lack of other devices in the area?

messages.reply_html:
Mike's Allotment
UK legal limit is 27dBm - which is why most LoRa chips limit tx power to 22dB to allow +5dB antenna gain. The Heltec V4s tx at above the UK legal limit, and that's even without any antenna gain. I guess you're going to be unlucky if someone complains and you get caught... but you take your chances etc. ....

messages.author: likkleidiot_nwo
messages.text: Would an 8.5 or an 11dbi be overkill if you don't have a clear LOS on an hill some place. I had a 5.8dbi on Crows nest 1 on the moors and replaced it with a 3dbi. I calculated the 3dbi hits the max distance I can hit without using a 10-20m mast..I almost bought a 8dbi to try hit Anglesey but the curve of the earth won't allow unless I build this mast. Also had no issue with Chinese anttena so far..tested and all the ones I've bought have been tuned for 868 .. the ones I bought off Amazon were sold as 868 but tinned to 915
messages.content_html: Would an 8.5 or an 11dbi be overkill if you don't have a clear LOS on an hill some place. I had a 5.8dbi on Crows nest 1 on the moors and replaced it with a 3dbi. I calculated the 3dbi hits the max distance I can hit without using a 10-20m mast..I almost bought a 8dbi to try hit Anglesey but the curve of the earth won't allow unless I build this mast. Also had no issue with Chinese anttena so far..tested and all the ones I've bought have been tuned for 868 .. the ones I bought off Amazon were sold as 868 but tinned to 915

messages.author: likkleidiot_nwo
messages.text: I'm quite lucky as live 5k from 520m high moorland, which looks out into my valley and across Manchester. I guess your first step is looking on a topo map at the high points around you...use the tools in Mesh core to check line of sight and where the estimated area your repeater would cover. They ain't 100% but you should be able to find a spot maybe that a repeater could go and see you and a neighbouring repeater. Can take lots of trial and error but better than having to hike 5k up a 500m high moorland
messages.content_html: I'm quite lucky as live 5k from 520m high moorland, which looks out into my valley and across Manchester. I guess your first step is looking on a topo map at the high points around you...use the tools in Mesh core to check line of sight and where the estimated area your repeater would cover. They ain't 100% but you should be able to find a spot maybe that a repeater could go and see you and a neighbouring repeater. Can take lots of trial and error but better than having to hike 5k up a 500m high moorland

messages.author: boychicken71
messages.text: Evening, I wasn't sure the best place to ask this. But here goes, so I now have some 235 contacts listed, 176 of these are repeaters and I constantly see messages in the public channel. I've sent messages in this channel but no idea if anyone ever receives them. I've tried to send messages directly to users in my contact list but they always fail and I'm trying to understand why. When I look at my contact map and try to ping the closet repeater it always fails as well.
messages.content_html: Evening, I wasn't sure the best place to ask this. But here goes, so I now have some 235 contacts listed, 176 of these are repeaters and I constantly see messages in the public channel. I've sent messages in this channel but no idea if anyone ever receives them. I've tried to send messages directly to users in my contact list but they always fail and I'm trying to understand why. When I look at my contact map and try to ping the closet repeater it always fails as well.

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: When you send a message in public or a private channel, do you hear any repeats? If repeat acknowledgements are not showing its possible you are not getting out. If they are, look at where the repeats are coming from on a map. Does it suggest you go are going far enough to reach other people? Are you using the right #northwest channel? (not #yorkshire just seen you are on the other side) public could be anywhere and possibly asymmetric where they cannot hear but the can send to your area.
messages.content_html: When you send a message in public or a private channel, do you hear any repeats? If repeat acknowledgements are not showing its possible you are not getting out. If they are, look at where the repeats are coming from on a map. Does it suggest you go are going far enough to reach other people? Are you using the right #northwest channel? (not #yorkshire just seen you are on the other side) public could be anywhere and possibly asymmetric where they cannot hear but the can send to your area.

messages.author: mowcius
messages.text: This means that you've only heard a repeat from your own repeater (but it also is aware of a second one with the same first two bytes) As you've not got direct LOS (your loft is in the way, but also from memory there are hills between you and your closest repeaters), you're probably not consistently receiving messages either. It might be that some of your messages out are getting places, but you're not getting the repeated message back when it's been sent.
messages.content_html: This means that you've only heard a repeat from your own repeater (but it also is aware of a second one with the same first two bytes) As you've not got direct LOS (your loft is in the way, but also from memory there are hills between you and your closest repeaters), you're probably not consistently receiving messages either. It might be that some of your messages out are getting places, but you're not getting the repeated message back when it's been sent.

It might be that some of your messages out are getting places, but you're not getting the repeated message back when it's been sent.

Do I splash out on a better aerial and that reminds me I really should buy some cases for them heltecs

messages.author: boychicken71
messages.text: So I am now spent up for the month which is a bit worrying since it doesn't start until tomorrow ... but I've bought the XIAO board and the LoRa Module to go with it (from links provided above) and a PiHut 868 Lora antenna with pigtail (£25 delivered all in). There's a house in Lofthouse I can put this along with my USB power bank. From memory I could just reach my repeater from there with my companion the other week and that was when I was in my car. So I'm thinking if I have two repeaters elevated, well one in the loft at my house and the other in Lofthouse in the bedroom it might just work. I've checked my coverage multiple times and it looks good. (edited)

XIAO ESP32S3 & Wio-SX1262 Kit for Meshtastic & LoRa
This is an ideal starter kit for any Meshtastic and LoRa/LoRaWAN projects! The kit combines the XIAO ESP32S3 microcontroller with the Wio-SX1262 LoRa module to add Wi-Fi, Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE), and LoRa, which opens up the possibilities to what you can create next! This added wireless connectivity makes it ideal for various Internet of Thin...

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: Do you have a shorter cable to try, u.fl to n-type or sma to n-type? Get rid of the long coax first then try some zero hop pings. if you get reasonable responses, which are not necessarily going to be much better than the little antenna, you can assume for now it is ok and look at the cable. (edited)
messages.content_html: Do you have a shorter cable to try, u.fl to n-type or sma to n-type? Get rid of the long coax first then try some zero hop pings. if you get reasonable responses, which are not necessarily going to be much better than the little antenna, you can assume for now it is ok and look at the cable. (edited)

messages.author: mowcius
messages.text: If you can't get the antenna outside, then away from the wall would also be a bit better if you can work out where your closest repeaters are then you can try and position it so the least absorbent materials are in the way. As well as the dangling second coax that's not ideal, that window pane has metal spacers, and the corner of your wall most likely has a metal corner trim that has been plastered over - all of which are bad to be so close to the antenna.
messages.content_html: If you can't get the antenna outside, then away from the wall would also be a bit better if you can work out where your closest repeaters are then you can try and position it so the least absorbent materials are in the way. As well as the dangling second coax that's not ideal, that window pane has metal spacers, and the corner of your wall most likely has a metal corner trim that has been plastered over - all of which are bad to be so close to the antenna.

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: I have had contacts 600 miles away via a mountain peak in Switzerland. Unfortunately I lost most of the details and never got the path taken but this is the one. As it was visible for many hours I would have expected to see adverts from France or Germany but never did. Only UK and Netherlands. This was helped by the atmospheric conditions to overcome the curvature but proves that distance isn't really the issue. (edited)

messages.author: oldfart0089

messages.content_html: all the solar nodes are rak4631 based, right? they look surprisingly spendy on aliexpress, like £35 with just an antenna and no battery/solar connector, vs under £20 for a heltec v3?

messages.author: G5TAR - Mike
messages.text: The RAK boards are generally easier to work with due to the dedicated solar input etc but I also run solar repeaters using Xiao nRF boards and a separate MPPT. The Xiaos are only about £13. ESP based boards like the Heltecs aren’t great for solar nodes unless you get a panel / battery big enough to support it
messages.content_html: The RAK boards are generally easier to work with due to the dedicated solar input etc but I also run solar repeaters using Xiao nRF boards and a separate MPPT. The Xiaos are only about £13. ESP based boards like the Heltecs aren’t great for solar nodes unless you get a panel / battery big enough to support it
messages.reply_html:

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: Heltec v3/v4 are ESP32 based. Great for home or something with a display and keyboard lke T-Deck, but with the current firmware this is much less efficient and far more powerfull than needed for a repeater. They are being clocked down to 80MHz but cannot go lower due to the BT and WIFI. (edited)
messages.content_html: Heltec v3/v4 are ESP32 based. Great for home or something with a display and keyboard lke T-Deck, but with the current firmware this is much less efficient and far more powerfull than needed for a repeater. They are being clocked down to 80MHz but cannot go lower due to the BT and WIFI. (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: Its a shame as the T1000 was so far ahead when launched but spoilt by firmware they had no control over, so people had a bad experience unless they ran the dev code on it. Now Rak https://docs.rakwireless.com/product-categories/meshtastic/wismesh-tag/overview/ resells someone else's product https://www.fccinsights.com/moko-technology-limited/2AO94-LW010 / https://www.mokosmart.com/lw010-ct-lorawan-card-tracker/ which is almost the same, so it benefits from all the development done for the T1000. If you want something similar with less risk of breaking the Rak has a slight stronger, bigger case and battery but it needs that for a more power hungry GPS which gets the initial lock faster. Side by side with the latest code they are functionally identical. (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: A cat collar unfortunately wont ever work for this. It could be made maybe half the size but only with a fraction of the battery capacity. It would need a custom board and firmware to work with those restrictions and even that size is a danger to a cat unless it can slip the collar, so you loose the device. The antenna has to work in all orientations so it will always be quite weak at transmitting. Now attach this to a body 20cm off the ground and it practically wont work at all. There are already RF solutions which work up to 100m, or similar sized Apple / Google BT/UWB tags. If you have seen this done on tv, they gathered GPS and image data on a device, then retrieved it later. A dog is different, it can easily go in one of those harness things. (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: One solution might be custom firmware on a BT tag which allows multiple beacons, so it works on both Apple and Google systems. I'm sure I saw some work in this direction on Airtags but it was probably stopped by Apple making changes to the firmware. Now that Google versions are available it might be possible as they wont all be locked down. https://www.hackster.io/news/leon-bottger-s-experimental-firmware-turns-any-espressif-esp32-into-a-google-find-my-device-tag-0cb0ea9c08c1 Back to the original point, if you are prepared to put something PetTracer sized on your cat this is completely possible and would not actually be that hard. A Xiao WIO nRF + GPS has three small square boards. Instead of stacking them, link them on the same plane with some flexible circuit board so now it can curve. Add a small battery or two, positioned so the weight oriented the collar in a particular way then if possible to arrange the right distance, two small flexible antenna making use of this to position themselves at the 10:00/2:00 (60 and 300 deg). Then custom firmware to store GPS and schedule a private channel message or to attempt again when a message or advert is received as it may have coma back into range. Make use of the IMU in the XIAO nRF52840 Sense to control the GPS. It should only run to get a fix on start, on a schedule and when the IMU detect significant movement. If you have enough repeaters covering the ground It might work. If you have gone this far, integrate the apple/google tag beacon code into this firmware as well... (edited)
messages.content_html: One solution might be custom firmware on a BT tag which allows multiple beacons, so it works on both Apple and Google systems. A Xiao WIO nRF + GPS has three small square boards. Instead of stacking them, link them on the same plane with some flexible circuit board so now it can curve. Add a small battery or two, positioned so the weight oriented the collar in a particular way then if possible to arrange the right distance, two small flexible antenna making use of this to position themselves at the 10:00/2:00 (60 and 300 deg). Then custom firmware to store GPS and schedule a private channel message or to attempt again when a message or advert is received as it may have coma back into range. Make use of the IMU in the XIAO nRF52840 Sense to control the GPS. It should only run to get a fix on start, on a schedule and when the IMU detect significant movement. If you have enough repeaters covering the ground It might work. If you have gone this far, integrate the apple/google tag beacon code into this firmware as well... (edited)

messages.author: apt
messages.text: Hi all, having issues with my solar node not coming back up after loosing power. The setup has a Seeed XIAO nRF52840 with an all in one MPPT and battery. I can see the battery has power and is fully charged. The led it on for the XIAO but it’s not reachable. Pressing and holding the reset button on the XIAO wakes it and I can then reconnect. Anyone got any idea what may be causing this issue? (edited)
messages.content_html: Hi all, having issues with my solar node not coming back up after loosing power. The setup has a Seeed XIAO nRF52840 with an all in one MPPT and battery. I can see the battery has power and is fully charged. The led it on for the XIAO but it’s not reachable. Pressing and holding the reset button on the XIAO wakes it and I can then reconnect. Anyone got any idea what may be causing this issue? (edited)

messages.text: It’s this one https://amzn.eu/d/6xHL8tp and using a 18650 with it. The battery shows as charged just the node not coming back online.

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: The board may have got into a situation where it has crashed due to low power input, rather than completely shutting down. Then remained in that state when the power increased. More battery capacity would make this happen less often. (edited)
messages.content_html: The board may have got into a situation where it has crashed due to low power input, rather than completely shutting down. Then remained in that state when the power increased. More battery capacity would make this happen less often. (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: I can pick up a signal from LS27-RPT at Aldi roundabout, getting stronger at ATS roundabout. From about Morley Academy across to Morley Cricket Club I could communicate back through it to a device in my house. Once I got up the Chinese takeaways and Asda it is really strong. messages.author: markab
messages.text: Do you still need to build a custom firmware to do this?
messages.content_html: Do you still need to build a custom firmware to do this?
messages.reply_html:
Mike's Allotment
No - the max permitted power emanating from your antenna must not exceed 27dBm. This includes any gain provided by your antenna. So ... if you have a 5dB antenna then your transmitter (LoRa chip in our case) can tx max 22dB ( 22 + 5 = 27). If your LoRa chip tx power is 22dB then you can add a max 5dB antenna to take it up to the max permitted tx power from the antenna of 27db The Heltec V4 has an internal LNA, and has been shown to tx at 29db (see Andy's video). That already exceeds that max 27db permitted by UK regs ... and that's before you even add on any gain provided by your antenna. The MC default firmware for the Heltec V4 limits the tx power from the chip to 22dB - however you can build your own firmware to increase the tx power to the max 29dB that the V4 can actually generate (illegally in the UK) - again - see Andy's video.

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: No the max the chip will accept is 22 on any board. On a v4 the default is 10, which then gets further amplified by another component you cannot control to become 22. If you increase that to 22 it becomes about 28 depending on frequency. (edited)
messages.content_html: No the max the chip will accept is 22 on any board. On a v4 the default is 10, which then gets further amplified by another component you cannot control to become 22. If you increase that to 22 it becomes about 28 depending on frequency. (edited)

embeds_html:
Fix GC1109 FEM LNA being used for Heltec v4 RX by weebl2000 ·...
I was experiencing very bad RX with my heltec v4 so I investigated and debugged. Turns out everything handling the LNA/PA is completely fine, the only problem was the SX1262 boosted gain was enable...

messages.reply_html:
mrlm
I have been inthis situation for months with heltec v3 and v4. Most of the time the amped v4 seems to work better when tested but it is clearly not right, and I dont know where the issue is, hardware or firmware (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: @weebl2000 I tried disabling boosted gain and it did nothing. I must have done something wrong in the code and it didn't disable. I will have a look at how you did it and test. Did the noise floor ever go to -120 for you? Mine seems to do it once every couple weeks, and I can see it fluctuating between -112 and -127. It is normally during the night when I'm asleep so I could never catch what caused it but I did notice that testing another repeater in the same house made it far worse, so no amount of fancy cavity filters would help. Ok a lot of reading there and its different to the title, seems like I was thinking the same as you looking at that table to trying to see how the chip could bypassed and controlled the PA / LNA but had zero chance of figuring out what you eventually did. (edited)

messages.author: James - M7MMD
messages.text: Cheers! I didn't tune as all the kit was at my work, whilst my NanoVNA was at home, so only tested before fitting to make sure it wasn't horrendous. It's a bit of a faff to get to now I've fitted it, so not sure if I'm incentivised enough to go up and tune. It'd be nice to know what the real world difference would be before and after tuning, but I guess that's a near impossible question and I don't have another repeater in range to benchmark from. If someone else pops up with a repeater within reach then I may then be inclined to tinker! For now, at least it's up and running!
messages.content_html: Cheers! I didn't tune as all the kit was at my work, whilst my NanoVNA was at home, so only tested before fitting to make sure it wasn't horrendous. messages.author: Phil
messages.text: Thinking of putting a couple of repeaters up on some radio ham repeater sites I look after in the Air Valley around Bradford. What is the most reliable unit to use as access to the sites is difficult so I need something I can fit and forget.
messages.content_html: Thinking of putting a couple of repeaters up on some radio ham repeater sites I look after in the Air Valley around Bradford. What is the most reliable unit to use as access to the sites is difficult so I need something I can fit and forget.

messages.author: G5TAR - Mike
messages.text: RAK 19007 / 4631 combos are my go-to. Reliable boards and have a Bluetooth IPEX connector so you can add a decent BT antenna for OTA updates. I put one up at the GB3WF repeater site with that build and can update it over Bluetooth from the roadside. It’s got a 10,000mah battery, 6w solar panel, low temp charge cutoff and and AHT10 sensor for temp / humidity to give me a clue if water gets inside. Looks a bit like this one but is pole mount instead of mag mount.
messages.content_html: RAK 19007 / 4631 combos are my go-to. Reliable boards and have a Bluetooth IPEX connector so you can add a decent BT antenna for OTA updates. I put one up at the GB3WF repeater site with that build and can update it over Bluetooth from the roadside. It’s got a 10,000mah battery, 6w solar panel, low temp charge cutoff and and AHT10 sensor for temp / humidity to give me a clue if water gets inside. Looks a bit like this one but is pole mount instead of mag mount.

messages.text: Do you have a parts list? Looks like a solid build. (edited)
messages.content_html: Do you have a parts list? Looks like a solid build. (edited)
messages.reply_html:
G5TAR - Mike
RAK 19007 / 4631 combos are my go-to. Reliable boards and have a Bluetooth IPEX connector so you can add a decent BT antenna for OTA updates. I put one up at the GB3WF repeater site with that build and can update it over Bluetooth from the roadside. It’s got a 10,000mah battery, 6w solar panel, low temp charge cutoff and and AHT10 sensor for temp / humidity to give me a clue if water gets inside. Looks a bit like this one but is pole mount instead of mag mount.

messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: Just a heads-up – I’ve been tinkering with something called MeshRank It’s basically a way of giving a bit more visibility into what the mesh is actually doing, without changing how MeshCORE works. No internet routing, no messing with RF, nothing in the message path. It just listens, observes, and feeds back what already happened. Why I built it: • New repeaters often get switched off because it’s hard to tell if they’re helping • People end up spamming test messages just to see if anything’s alive • When stuff doesn’t propagate, there’s no clue why MeshRank gives things like delivery confidence, shows which repeaters are contributing, and highlights where messages are being seen but not going any further. Observers are totally passive — they don’t transmit or interfere in any way. If MeshRank fell over tomorrow, the mesh would carry on exactly the same. I’m looking for a few folks (UK first) who wouldn’t mind running an observer and helping build some early coverage — high ground / urban spots especially useful. If you fancy a look: https://meshrank.net Happy to answer questions or take criticism
messages.content_html: Just a heads-up – I’ve been tinkering with something called MeshRank It’s basically a way of giving a bit more visibility into what the mesh is actually doing, without changing how MeshCORE works. No internet routing, no messing with RF, nothing in the message path. It just listens, observes, and feeds back what already happened. Why I built it: • New repeaters often get switched off because it’s hard to tell if they’re helping • People end up spamming test messages just to see if anything’s alive • When stuff doesn’t propagate, there’s no clue why MeshRank gives things like delivery confidence, shows which repeaters are contributing, and highlights where messages are being seen but not going any further. Observers are totally passive — they don’t transmit or interfere in any way. If MeshRank fell over tomorrow, the mesh would carry on exactly the same. I’m looking for a few folks (UK first) who wouldn’t mind running an observer and helping build some early coverage — high ground / urban spots especially useful. If you fancy a look: https://meshrank.net Happy to answer questions or take criticism

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: Be careful buying random antenna on Amazon, some are not great and they are all more expensive than they should be. 860MHz to 930MHz is a massive range. Any decent antenna like that narrows it down to a few Mhz. RG58 is terrible cable for this application, but you are not using it. I have one from Aliexpress which is fine but you can get good quality ones in the UK if you know where to look. 6dBI Helium Antenna Tuned McGill Microwave High Gain N Male EU 868 Hotspot Miner £29.99or Best Offer - they have accepted £15 before. https://ebay.us/m/wlgDEB (edited)
messages.content_html: Be careful buying random antenna on Amazon, some are not great and they are all more expensive than they should be. 860MHz to 930MHz is a massive range. Any decent antenna like that narrows it down to a few Mhz. RG58 is terrible cable for this application, but you are not using it. I have one from Aliexpress which is fine but you can get good quality ones in the UK if you know where to look. 6dBI Helium Antenna Tuned McGill Microwave High Gain N Male EU 868 Hotspot Miner £29.99or Best Offer - they have accepted £15 before. https://ebay.us/m/wlgDEB (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: 6 miles is nothing, any device with working antenna can cover that. The T1000e and Rak Tag are card sized trackers with omni directional antenna, so almost no directional gain. As I am on top of a hill they reliably work 20 miles to repeaters also on hills. The question is whether the angle the higher gain covers is appropriate and whether the extra cost will have any benefit. https://www.mcgillmicrowave.com/3-dbi-tuned-antenna-eu-868/ 56 +/- 1 degrees (3dBi) signal radiation footprint. https://www.mcgillmicrowave.com/6-dbi-tuned-antenna-eu-868/ 35 +/- 1 degrees (6dBi) signal radiation footprint. (edited)
messages.content_html: 6 miles is nothing, any device with working antenna can cover that. The T1000e and Rak Tag are card sized trackers with omni directional antenna, so almost no directional gain. As I am on top of a hill they reliably work 20 miles to repeaters also on hills. The question is whether the angle the higher gain covers is appropriate and whether the extra cost will have any benefit. https://www.mcgillmicrowave.com/3-dbi-tuned-antenna-eu-868/ 56 +/- 1 degrees (3dBi) signal radiation footprint. https://www.mcgillmicrowave.com/6-dbi-tuned-antenna-eu-868/ 35 +/- 1 degrees (6dBi) signal radiation footprint. (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: https://buymeacoffee.com/ripplebiz/meshcore-update-6 The packet data is collected by observer nodes (can be either repeater or companion nodes). Is your observer firmware custom or based on an existing role? I know its not exactly the same but could it run on a modified repeater or companion rather than having two devices? Heltec v3/4 are clocked down from 240Mhz to 80Mhz if more resources were needed. I think I asked this the other day. (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: Being by the sea is a pain as you are inevitably near sea level and below everything inland. From looking at the map, assuming the location is reasonably near where you actually are, you can only go along the coast NW. I would go to the top of that 170m hill and see if you can do any better. If you can maybe a repeater somewhere around there will be the answer. But the other nodes near there (Stockton) are patchy as well so local knowledge is far better than me guessing. (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: If you are really struggling for a location you can build a node which is just battery powered and will last a few months. I had the idea of hiding it all inside a black pvc tube (late discovering it would be best to leave the antenna out) which would be much easier to hide in plain sight than a box with solar etc. I can then be mounted in a less than ideal north facing tree if thats what is needed, untill a better site can be found. (edited)

messages.author: hawkins
messages.text: It won't let the battery charge under 5 degrees so the battery doesn't get damaged, reason I've set it to 5 is because the sun will heat the panel on the other side of the stat which means it'll get hotter than the batteries (if that makes sense) I drill a 2mm hole at the bottom to allow the pressure to equalise as temperatures change. It would also let water escape if it gets in (edited)

messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: Morning all — a few updates to MeshRank.net: Heltec V4 can now run as a combined Observer + Repeater (beta). An NTP time sync has been added once Wi-Fi connects, so the device always has the correct date/time after reboot. This also includes the Heltec V4 SX1262 / GC1109 RX fix. As this is beta, please don’t update the repeater that’s bolted to your roof just yet The MQTT feed is now open for anyone already using meshcoretomqtt (or similar) and wanting to mirror their packet stream into MeshRank. Advanced packet routing maps are nearly finished. These are based on mutual neighbour connections and can infer likely paths where there are gaps in the observed route. I’m also finalising MeshLive — a real-time visual view of the mesh, including country-wide noise floor, live routes, and packets per minute. This will be released to observers first.
messages.content_html: Morning all — a few updates to MeshRank.net: Heltec V4 can now run as a combined Observer + Repeater (beta). An NTP time sync has been added once Wi-Fi connects, so the device always has the correct date/time after reboot. This also includes the Heltec V4 SX1262 / GC1109 RX fix. As this is beta, please don’t update the repeater that’s bolted to your roof just yet The MQTT feed is now open for anyone already using meshcoretomqtt (or similar) and wanting to mirror their packet stream into MeshRank. Advanced packet routing maps are nearly finished. These are based on mutual neighbour connections and can infer likely paths where there are gaps in the observed route. I’m also finalising MeshLive — a real-time visual view of the mesh, including country-wide noise floor, live routes, and packets per minute. This will be released to observers first.

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: I had odd issues last night updating the Repeater-Observer firmware. Eevery time I flashed and sent the config it seemd to work but nothing changed. Probably because of things I have done in the past like use the companion firmware to backup the repeater key so there was unexpected config present. The process on the site worked after I did this - which is basically how you fix most things. Backup your repeater private key with the Meshcore web site console. https://flasher.meshcore.co.uk/ Erase the device with the Meshcore site flasher. Upload the repeater firmware with the Meshcore site flasher. Restore the key with the Meshcore web site console. Then go to https://meshrank.net / Become an Observer / Repeater and continue. This creates a new observer and repeater on the site and as far as I can tell it is working as it did before and going back up the ranking. (edited)
messages.content_html: I had odd issues last night updating the Repeater-Observer firmware. Eevery time I flashed and sent the config it seemd to work but nothing changed. Probably because of things I have done in the past like use the companion firmware to backup the repeater key so there was unexpected config present. The process on the site worked after I did this - which is basically how you fix most things. Backup your repeater private key with the Meshcore web site console. https://flasher.meshcore.co.uk/ Erase the device with the Meshcore site flasher. Upload the repeater firmware with the Meshcore site flasher. Restore the key with the Meshcore web site console. Then go to https://meshrank.net / Become an Observer / Repeater and continue. This creates a new observer and repeater on the site and as far as I can tell it is working as it did before and going back up the ranking. (edited)

messages.author: S7uart G5AIL
messages.text: One of the biggest challenges with MeshCore is the dreaded Hashbyte issue. Repeaters identify themselves with a 2 digit code. It actually doesn’t affect the mesh. But makes it difficult to work out exactly which route a message has taken. I’ve added a repeater ID clashes page to the site and added a personalised review for each repeater on the repeater rank. So if you have a close neighbour sharing the same code it helps you pick a safe option. You can then log into your repeater and update the code in Tools. Check it out on Meshrank.net Hope it helps. (edited)
messages.content_html: One of the biggest challenges with MeshCore is the dreaded Hashbyte issue. Repeaters identify themselves with a 2 digit code. It actually doesn’t affect the mesh. But makes it difficult to work out exactly which route a message has taken. I’ve added a repeater ID clashes page to the site and added a personalised review for each repeater on the repeater rank. So if you have a close neighbour sharing the same code it helps you pick a safe option. You can then log into your repeater and update the code in Tools. Check it out on Meshrank.net Hope it helps. (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: I had an Idea how this should be done using three geographic levels but didn't see anyone discussing it much yet. I recently spotted this post which goes into a lot of detail and suggests using predefined ISO codes for the areas. https://discord.com/channels/1343693475589263471/1391673743453192242/1477261545581969438 This solves problem which people are probably not thinking about, there needs to be some kind of standard naming convention for regions or it wont work.
messages.content_html: I had an Idea how this should be done using three geographic levels but didn't see anyone discussing it much yet. I recently spotted this post which goes into a lot of detail and suggests using predefined ISO codes for the areas. https://discord.com/channels/1343693475589263471/1391673743453192242/1477261545581969438 This solves problem which people are probably not thinking about, there needs to be some kind of standard naming convention for regions or it wont work.
messages.reply_html:
G5TAR - Mike
There are some test regions configured on a handful of the repeaters but nobody has disabled the global region as far as I’m aware so shouldn’t change anything

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: I would be tempted to hide a tracker at least temporarily in any new deployments in case it gets stolen or moved. Once you know a site is safe you can remove it an use it elsewhere. Airtags are a bit too expensive and apple only. You can get cheaper but I can recommend these which cost £7.50 each, can work on either and need charging once a year https://www.amazon.co.uk/UGREEN-FineTrack-Rechargeable-Compatible-Backpack-dp-B0FRLWSGPW/dp/B0FRLWSGPW/ref=dp_ob_title_ce (edited)

messages.author: G5TAR - Mike
messages.text: Yeah that’s cool - it’s the thing I enjoy most about the mesh is building the repeaters . The one in the pic has 10,000mah battery and it’s just replaced one with a 5,200mah battery. The 5,200 mah JUST gave in towards the back end of winter so I’m confident the 10,000 will be fine this year . It’s got a slightly bigger panel and dedicated mppt charge controller too, plus shallow angle of the panel to catch the winter sun better
messages.content_html: Yeah that’s cool - it’s the thing I enjoy most about the mesh is building the repeaters . The one in the pic has 10,000mah battery and it’s just replaced one with a 5,200mah battery. The 5,200 mah JUST gave in towards the back end of winter so I’m confident the 10,000 will be fine this year . It’s got a slightly bigger panel and dedicated mppt charge controller too, plus shallow angle of the panel to catch the winter sun better

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: This is inside the back of the D5 Solar. The panel connects to the bottom left and charges the batteries in parallel. Depending on the model you will get different voltages out of the bottom port and the internal port on the bottom right. I have the 5v version so I just wired a usb port from there to the board. There are two points at the top for antennas or a cable port you can drill out. (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: I am not sure where I bought mine but it was probably here https://www.cellsupply.co.uk/18650-batteries I would normally say stick with Japanese and Korean brands but some of these others will be fine. You don't need ridiculous amps and stay away from anything claiming more than 3500mAh, it is physically impossible to fit 4000 in this size cell. Make sure all batteries are balanced to within 0.05v before you connect them together in the device, by charging them all separately then check voltage. It is much more dangerous when one of them is empty as it will take as much current as it can from the other batteries. Once they are balanced they should stay that way. Flat tops as pictured are a pain and really intended for spot welding together, I had to make it work by bending the metal slightly but button tops would have worked immediately. (edited)
messages.content_html: I am not sure where I bought mine but it was probably here https://www.cellsupply.co.uk/18650-batteries I would normally say stick with Japanese and Korean brands but some of these others will be fine. You don't need ridiculous amps and stay away from anything claiming more than 3500mAh, it is physically impossible to fit 4000 in this size cell. Make sure all batteries are balanced to within 0.05v before you connect them together in the device, by charging them all separately then check voltage. It is much more dangerous when one of them is empty as it will take as much current as it can from the other batteries. Once they are balanced they should stay that way. Flat tops as pictured are a pain and really intended for spot welding together, I had to make it work by bending the metal slightly but button tops would have worked immediately. (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: V4 has an extra amplifier chip after the normal radio. It needs special commands to control it. V4.3 changed the chip to a new one and needs different commands. The radio was transmitting but the RF switch in the amp was stuck in RX.
messages.content_html: V4 has an extra amplifier chip after the normal radio. It needs special commands to control it. V4.3 changed the chip to a new one and needs different commands. The radio was transmitting but the RF switch in the amp was stuck in RX.

messages.author: G5TAR - Mike
messages.text: I think the biggest challenge with something like that would be the building insurance if the repeater battery goes up in flames and where the liability lies. I try and get agreement from landowners who have telegraph poles on their land in high spots. If you deploy with a ladder someone would also need a ladder to steal it so rules out opportunists. Hardest bit is giving them enough of an understanding of the project that they’re not dubious of it.

messages.author: Undead Zoidberg
messages.text: Afternoon Yorkshire mesh! Am a newbie, got my meshcore connected last night. Had some questions if anyone is able to help illuminate me on some general things Any links to YouTube videos would also be appreciated if typing a load of text is unfun Question 1) How do you tend to use your channels best? Do you have private channels set up with friends/family/community? Or do you have private channels you connect to with strangers online like a chat room? The Public channel is cool but doesn't seem efficient for chatting! And no one is talking on #chat (or at least I'm not picking anyone up with my weak antenna) Question 2) What is it about the nature of radio that means that not everyone in the UK is all hearing each other on the public channel? Surely there's enough repeaters in the UK that everyone could hear each other all the time? Maybe I'm just overestimating the power of radio signals. Do the radio waves disintegrate over time from too many hops (if say Scotland is failing to chat to England)? (edited)
messages.content_html: Afternoon Yorkshire mesh! Am a newbie, got my meshcore connected last night. Had some questions if anyone is able to help illuminate me on some general things Any links to YouTube videos would also be appreciated if typing a load of text is unfun Question 1) How do you tend to use your channels best? The Public channel is cool but doesn't seem efficient for chatting! And no one is talking on #chat (or at least I'm not picking anyone up with my weak antenna) Question 2) What is it about the nature of radio that means that not everyone in the UK is all hearing each other on the public channel? Surely there's enough repeaters in the UK that everyone could hear each other all the time? Maybe I'm just overestimating the power of radio signals. Do the radio waves disintegrate over time from too many hops (if say Scotland is failing to chat to England)? (edited)

messages.author: Fireball
messages.text: There are a few different channels depending on interests and areas, e.g Yorkshire, hamradio, jokes, test, northeast etc so very much depends what you want from it. Use case is whatever you fancy, I’m thinking of setting up a bot to pm me temperatures from the reptiles on set intervals for example (I could do it online too but in theory this works without data which will be handy while in low signal) As for signal it’s very much dependent on conditions, the radios are very low power so easily blocked and interfered with. Traffic light systems and whatnot are on similar frequencies so can deafen you.
messages.content_html: There are a few different channels depending on interests and areas, e.g Yorkshire, hamradio, jokes, test, northeast etc so very much depends what you want from it. messages.author: mowcius
messages.text: 868MHz is pretty dependent on reasonable LOS if you want to go more than a couple of miles. It might seem like there are lots of repeaters around, but it's nowhere near enough for there not to be any dead spots across the country yet.
messages.content_html: 868MHz is pretty dependent on reasonable LOS if you want to go more than a couple of miles. It might seem like there are lots of repeaters around, but it's nowhere near enough for there not to be any dead spots across the country yet.

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: If you wonder how you are recieving it is because as soon as you don't have line of sight it becomes very asymmetric, so getting weak signal in from a good location is easier than getting out of a poor one.

messages.author: Undead Zoidberg
messages.text: Has anyone got any case studies of people in UK using the mesh to report emergencies? One of my intentions is to have a mesh radio with me in case I'm hiking and stranded somewhere, or in the countryside where rescue needs to be called for and there's no signal. Probably the best strategy would be to announce something on the emergency hash? I've got a weak antenna for the moment so I've never actually heard any messages on the emergency hash Anyone seen any emergency situations resolved using mesh tech? (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: 3d printing waterproof is very hard and almost away not worth it, just buy a simple plastic box to hold things which need to stay dry, and print the complex shapes that dont like the panel holder. You are on the right track with the clear coat idea though, there are special liquids you dip a print into to fill the gaps. I'm on my phone but it thinks this is it https://diamant-polymer.de/en/shop/dichtol/am-hydro/ (edited)

messages.author: mrlm
messages.text: My idea for the water resistant companion case was around making it modular. A unique carrier for different boards, slots in a central frame . Different fronts for the display or buttons and back for various batteries clamp on with a silicone bead. Different antenna mounting modules on the side would alter the position, type and maybe allow rotation of the antenna when laid flat. Extend the usb port from the board to a waterproof one on the frame which can be sealed off when not in use. (edited)
messages.content_html: My idea for the water resistant companion case was around making it modular. A unique carrier for different boards, slots in a central frame . Different fronts for the display or buttons and back for various batteries clamp on with a silicone bead. Different antenna mounting modules on the side would alter the position, type and maybe allow rotation of the antenna when laid flat. Extend the usb port from the board to a waterproof one on the frame which can be sealed off when not in use. (edited)

messages.author: Khanlaar
messages.text: There shouldn't be any issues with your TV antenna being right there. USB wires are very thin and may not provide proper power at that distance. Keep the run as short as possible.

messages.author: EricT
messages.text: Re: Selby YO8. Hi, very recently joined MeshCore at Selby in a fringe area between major urban centres. I was hoping to contact Selby Repeater (dc0ecf6e) over on the north side of the river, actually in Barlby (53.7958, -1.0446, maybe up a lamppost at a road junction), but the signal was weak for me, and it seems to have gone AWOL these last few days. Selby has York to the north, Doncaster to the south, Castleford and Leeds to the west, and Hull to the east. Thankfully, I can easily receive from the excellent YM-SB-RPT (5676fea7) way up high at Sutton Bank, and less easily receive YM-GH-RPT (a8ab2fd0) on the A166 east of York near Bishop Wilton, even though these are distant contacts, and even with just a T-Deck Plus companion. Noise floor is good. The land here is flat as a pancake for miles around, so even Selby to Sutton Bank is only around a 1-degree elevation over that distance. However, transmission is more miss than hit, and I'm not in a position to install a loft or external repeater (living in a flat in a grade 2 listed building), unless there are any local veterans/hams with whom I could collaborate. The best I can do for now is point a small test Yagi in a north-westerly to easterly direction and place a test omni in a first-floor window covering north-west to east, looking out over the riverside park, amphitheatre, and Westmill Foods (tall, major cell tower; the Abbey is to the south-south-west also overlooking the town). Is there any interest in extending the mesh in Selby and district? Or do you have any ideas for a newbie? Thanks in advance. (edited)

messages.text: What device are you using, just the T-Deck? Height is the most important thing but if you cannot access the loft, the best thing would be to build a cheap solar repeater and try in the garden if you have a clear view to the north. (edited)

messages.author: EricT
messages.text: Thanks guys. Yes, height is surely the main thing here. I have a T-Deck Plus as a companion, with what was misadvertised as a 10dBi whip antenna (17.5cm long) but probably falls far short of that, though it's longer and sturdier than the stock antenna; I have a Heltec v3 with the same antenna for an omni repeater; and a 5-element Yagi for a directional repeater. Funnily enough, I was just checking out the Parador site. My current 868MHz Yagi (from AliExpress) is like Parador's 5-element 9dBi, and I see Parador have an 8-element 12.5dBi but are waiting for new stock. Will check out the whip link you included, thanks hawkins. I'll go away and explore possibilities. I have a tall retort stand in the post that will at least allow me to mount the Yagi near the ceiling on the first floor. There's new double-glazing installed which may well be coated and present more of an obstacle than the old-fashioned single brick wall. Hah. If I was still working, I could maybe have borrowed some test equipment over the weekend. And I'll have to try negotiating with my fortunately-friendly landlord about erecting some form of mast outside, as you kindly suggest. Thanks again! (edited)

messages.author: markvbr
messages.text: Britain’s biggest mobile network operators have warned ministers they may be forced to ration access to phone signals and introduce surge pricing at peak times, as the war in Iran sends wholesale energy costs spiralling and Whitehall shuts the sector out of its flagship industrial support package. Among the measures being modelled behind closed doors are the throttling of data speeds, restricting access during periods of high demand, and charging customers a premium at peak times, a move that would mark a significant departure from the all-you-can-eat tariffs that have dominated the British mobile market for more than a decade. Voice calls and mobile data are expected to bear the brunt of any rationing, though fixed-line broadband services could also be affected. Senior industry figures have further cautioned that relentless cost pressures could see 5G rollout plans shelved, with jobs either cut outright or shifted overseas. https://bmmagazine.co.uk/news/mobile-signal-rationing-uk-telecoms-energy-costs/

Voice calls and mobile data are expected to bear the brunt of any rationing, though fixed-line broadband services could also be affected. embeds_html:
Mobile operators warn of signal rationing as energy costs spiral
UK mobile operators VodafoneThree, Virgin Media O2 and EE warn they may ration signal access and introduce surge pricing after being excluded from Rachel Reeves's energy support scheme.
